author: Arunkumar Palanisamy’s Post
text: 𝐃𝐨 𝐘𝐨𝐮 𝐀𝐜𝐭𝐮𝐚𝐥𝐥𝐲 𝐊𝐧𝐨𝐰 𝐖𝐡𝐚𝐭 𝐇𝐚𝐩𝐩𝐞𝐧𝐬 𝐁𝐞𝐭𝐰𝐞𝐞𝐧 𝐘𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐏𝐫𝐨𝐦𝐩𝐭 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐌𝐨𝐝𝐞𝐥'𝐬 𝐑𝐞𝐬𝐩𝐨𝐧𝐬𝐞? Every LLM like ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Llama runs on the same 5-step process underneath. Once you see it, the magic stops being magic and starts being engineering. 1. Tokenization: The model doesn't see words. It sees numbers. Text is split into tokens, each assigned a unique ID from the vocabulary. "Today is a beautiful day" becomes a sequence of integers. This translation layer between human language and math is where everything begins. 2. Embeddings: Tokens are converted into vectors that represent meaning. Similar words land close together in vector space "happy" and "joyful" cluster, "boats" and "ships" cluster. The geometry encodes the meaning. This is why the model "gets" similarity without being told. 3. Transformer Architecture: Token embeddings flow through multi-head attention, add-and-normalize, and feed-forward networks. Attention lets every token relate to every other token simultaneously. The 2017 "Attention Is All You Need" breakthrough. Every modern LLM is built on this exact pattern. 4. Deep Learning Layers: Input representation passes through Layer 1, 2, 3... through Layer N. Each layer refines understanding syntax in early layers, semantics in middle ones, abstract reasoning deeper. GPT-3 had 96 layers. GPT-4 has many more. Depth is where capability comes from. 5. Response Generation: The model produces a probability distribution across the entire vocabulary and samples the next token. "Outside" (0.32), "today" (0.21), "now" (0.11). That's the entire output mechanism. Predict, sample, repeat. Everything an LLM does writing, coding, reasoning, summarizing emerges from step 5 applied millions of times at scale. There's no separate reasoning module. It's prediction all the way down. Why does understanding this change how you read AI news? • "Bigger context window": more tokens at step 1. • "Better reasoning": deeper layers at step 4. • "Lower hallucinations": smarter sampling at step 5. • "New model": same architecture, more scale. The architecture doesn't change as models get bigger. They stack more layers, with bigger windows, on more data. Scale is the lever. The five steps stay the same. Which step was your biggest "aha" moment? ♻️ Repost this to help your network get started ➕ Follow Arunkumar for more #LLMs #DeepLearning #AIEngineering

author: Adarsh C.’s Post
text: DSA isn’t about solving 3,500 problems it’s about recognizing the right pattern at the right time. Master patterns first, and speed + confidence follow automatically. 🙌💯 Here are the Top 15 DSA Patterns you need to know. 1. Two Pointers Use: Use two pointers to traverse a structure or range. Examples: 3Sum, Container With Most Water 1. Sliding Window Use : Maintain a window of elements and slide it to find solution. Examples: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters 1. Fast & Slow Pointers Use two pointers at different speeds. Examples: Linked List Cycle, Middle of Linked List 1. Binary Search Use : Divide search space in half to find the answer. Examples: Search in Rotated Sorted Array 1. Binary Search on Answer Use: Binary search the possible answer space. Examples: Koko Eating Bananas, Minimum Days to Make Bouquets 1. Merge Intervals Use : Merge overlapping intervals to simplify. Examples: Merge Intervals, Insert Interval 1. Backtracking Use : Try all possible choices and backtrack. Examples: N-Queens, Sudoku Solver 1. DFS (Depth First Search) Use : Go deep in one path before backtracking. Examples: Number of Islands, Clone Graph 1. BFS (Breadth First Search) Use : Explore level by level using a queue. Examples: Shortest Path in Binary Matrix 1. Topological Sort Use : Linear ordering of tasks with dependencies. Examples: Course Schedule, Alien Dictionary 1. Dynamic Programming Use : Solve by breaking into subproblems and storing results. Examples: Climbing Stairs, Coin Change 1. Greedy Use : Make the best local choice at each step. Examples: Dijkstra, Union Find, Graph Coloring 1. Graph Use : Work with nodes and edges. Examples: Implement Trie, Word Search II 1. Tries (Prefix Tree) Use : Tree-based structure for strings. Examples: Implement Trie, Word Search II 1. Heap / Priority Queue Use: Use heap to efficiently get min/max. Examples: Kth Largest Element, Merge K Sorted Lists Kth Largest Element → Use a min-heap of size `k` to find the kth largest element in an array. Get the Cheatsheet in your DMs directly: https://lnkd.in/e-hJY5fG #interview #interviewpro #interviewprep #dsa #coding #git #java #adarshchetan Do follow for more such amazing stuff ❤️

author: Angel Thakral’s Post
text: An app used by millions of people was built by the Indian government… yet most Indians have never heard of it. You may have used it today. Without downloading it. Without even knowing its name. And what makes it remarkable: • Uber invested ₹60 crore in it. • Paytm invested ₹60 crore in it. • Zoho invested ₹70 crore in it. • State Bank of India, HDFC Bank, ICICI Bank, Axis Bank, and Kotak Life are backing it. Yet, most people don't know it exists. Because Open Network For Digital Commerce (ONDC) isn't another app. It's the invisible digital infrastructure powering transactions behind the scenes. Book a metro ticket on Uber? ONDC enables that transaction. Order groceries on Paytm? ONDC may be working in the background. More than 10 million metro rides have already been booked through ONDC on Uber alone. While most companies are building closed ecosystems, India built an open network where buyers and sellers can connect without being locked into a single platform. That's why some of India's biggest companies aren't trying to own it. They're investing to be part of it. Sometimes, the most powerful technology is the one you never see. Follow Angel Thakral Before today, had you heard of ONDC? #ONDC #DigitalIndia #StartupIndia #IndianStartups #Technology #Innovation #Business #FutureOfCommerce #IndiaTech #LinkedInIndia

author: Adarsh C.’s Post
text:
Don't overwhelm to learn SQL,🙌

SQL is only this much👇😇

1. Basic SQL Syntax:
SQL keywords
Data types
Operators
SQL statements (SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE)

2. Data Definition Language (DDL):
CREATE TABLE
ALTER TABLE
DROP TABLE
Truncate table

3. Data Manipulation Language (DML):
SELECT statement (SELECT, FROM, WHERE, ORDER BY, GROUP BY, HAVING, JOINs)
INSERT statement
UPDATE statement
DELETE statement

4. Aggregate Functions:
SUM, AVG, COUNT, MIN, MAX
GROUP BY clause
HAVING clause

5. Data Constraints:
Primary Key
Foreign Key
Unique
NOT NULL
CHECK

6. Joins:
INNER JOIN
LEFT JOIN
RIGHT JOIN
FULL OUTER JOIN
Self Join
Cross Join

7. Subqueries:
Types of subqueries (scalar, column, row, table)
Nested subqueries
Correlated subqueries

8. Advanced SQL Functions:
String functions (CONCAT, LENGTH, SUBSTRING, REPLACE, UPPER, LOWER)
Date and time functions (DATE, TIME, TIMESTAMP, DATEPART, DATEADD)
Numeric functions 

9. Views:
Creating views
Modifying views
Dropping views

10. Indexes:
Creating indexes
Using indexes for query optimization

11. Transactions:
ACID properties
Transaction management (BEGIN, COMMIT, ROLLBACK, SAVEPOINT)
Transaction isolation levels

12. Data Integrity and Security:
Data integrity constraints (referential integrity, entity integrity)
GRANT and REVOKE statements (granting and revoking permissions)
Database security best practices

13. Stored Procedures and Functions:
Creating stored procedures
Executing stored procedures
Creating functions
Using functions in queries

14. Performance Optimization:
Query optimization techniques (using indexes, optimizing joins, reducing subqueries)
Performance tuning best practices

15. Advanced SQL Concepts:

Recursive queries
Pivot and unpivot operations
Window functions (Row_number, rank, dense_rank, lead & lag)
CTEs (Common Table Expressions)
Dynamic SQL

#interview #interviewprep #interviewpro #dsa #coding #notes #adarshchetan
